session_notes.note: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
Morning after waking up. It's the hardest to resist. And when I'm stressed about the farm, like if the crops aren't doing well or something.
I guess it's worth a shot. Yes that's right. I'll try tomorrow morning.
I do some of those things already, like checking my tools, but not regularly. I usually just sit and smoke.
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
When my coworkers are smoking on site and they offer me one, that's when I usually take it. "Have one, yaar," they say, and it feels rude to refuse.
It's fine, nothing big. I just feel... ignored, like they're not really seeing me when I don't smoke along with them.
My wife... she tries to help, but she's always stressed out about money too. She doesn't smoke, but she can't really understand what it's like to give up something that's a part of my life.
I haven't really talked to her about it, no. I don't want to burden her with my problems, and I don't want her to feel guilty about not being able to help more.
I don't know... I've been doing this for so long, and every time I try to quit, it feels like I'm failing. I'm not sure if she'll think I'm weak or something if I ask for her help.

I think I was able to quit for a few days once, when I was working on a project away from my regular workplace. There, nobody smoked, so I didn't have that trigger. I managed for about three or four days, but then I went back to work and it was hard to resist.
Yeah, maybe that could work. But what if I'm at home and I'm craving to smoke? That's when I get really anxious.
I get restless, I pace around. I need something to do with my hands.
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
It helps, yeah. If I'm feeling stressed or lonely, it's easy to grab a bidi. It's a habit, been doing it so long. But it's not helping my breathing, my daughter says.

One time my daughter asked me to walk with her, she's a big help, you know, trying to get me to do things. We went to the park, just around the block. It was okay, I guess. I felt a bit... lighter, maybe. But then I came back home and still had a bidi.

Yeah, I think they did, a bit. I wasn't thinking about smoking as much. But, it came back when I got home, and it was like I was waiting for it, you know, like I'd forgotten for a little while, but then it was there again.

I don't know, it's hard to do something else when I wake up at 3 AM and can't sleep. That's when I first reach for a bidi, it's like a habit. Trying to do something else before that feels like I'm fighting it, and it's hard to change so much.

Drinking water, yeah... that's not a bad idea. I've started doing it sometimes, just to see if it works. But it's hard to remember to do it when I wake up in the middle of the night. It's still a bit of a struggle.
A sticky note... yeah, that might help. It's a small thing, but maybe it's enough. My daughter puts sticky notes everywhere, I guess it could work.

I think it would help if my daughter was there with me, reminding me. Or if I had someone to talk to, about my wife and everything. It's hard to remember to do something small when I'm thinking about everything else.

It's just... she was my everything, you know? We were together for so long, and then she passed away. I still wake up expecting to see her, and it's hard to sleep without her. It's like... everything feels so empty without her.
Writing things down... I've never tried that. It might help to get some of this out, yeah. Maybe I can write down things I'm grateful for, like my daughter, and that might help me feel a bit better.
I think so. Yeah, I'll try it. It's not a lot to do, and maybe it'll help. I'll put that sticky note with the water on the nightstand, and I'll write something down in the morning.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
Around 10, I guess. It's been 12 years now.
My son... seeing him get treatment here, it made me think about how long I've been smoking and how it might be affecting him. Maybe if I stopped smoking, I'd be a better example for him.
I don't know... I guess I'd just feel like I'm being a hypocrite or something. Like I'm trying to change, but my own son doesn't care.

That might be helpful, I guess. It's hard for me to think about right now, but if I had something to look at later, it might remind me why I wanted to quit in the first place. Like a checklist or something.

motivation_reason: fear of being seen as irresponsible

session_notes.note: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

She gets on my nerves, you know? She's always nagging me, saying I'm going to die from lung cancer or something. I just tell her to calm down and that I'm not going to listen to her. I don't want to hear it.

That's the worst. I get embarrassed, you know? Like, I'm a grown man, and she's still treating me like a kid. And in front of everyone else, it's like, what are they going to think? Like they're going to judge me or something.

I don't know, I just think they'd think I'm not a responsible person, you know? Like, if I can't even quit smoking with all the warnings and stuff, what else can't I do? They'd think I'm a failure or something.

Yeah... like when I'm stuck in the shop for a long time with nothing to do. It's boring, you know? And I always reach for a cigarette then. Or after tea in the morning, when I'm still waking up, a cigarette tastes good. And when customers start haggling, I get stressed and a cigarette helps calm me down.
Uh, I guess I could work on some papers or something. But it's hard, you know? It's boring too. And it's not like I need to do it. I just sit there waiting for customers, you know?
I don't know... maybe. Like, I could organize the shelves or something. But it's not going to make a difference, is it? I'll still sit there and get bored, and I'll still need a cigarette.
A game, yeah... that might make it more interesting. But what if I'm still bored afterwards?
Yeah, that could work. I mean, I could talk to my wife about it. She's always on my case about something, maybe she can come up with some ideas.
Maybe. I don't know... it's just that she's always been on my case about it, I don't know if she'll ever understand why I still smoke.

smoking_status_history: cigarettes for a whole pack a day
motivation_reason: be able to run with my kid
be able to play with my dog
take a deep breath without coughing

session_notes.note: I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

Well, it's like my mind just goes back to smoking. I'll be fine for a few days and then I'll have a bad day and just grab a cigarette to calm down. Before I know it, I've gone back to smoking a whole pack a day. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.

I don't know... it sounds cliche, but I guess I would feel more energetic and confident. I'd be able to run with my kid or play with my dog without getting winded. And I think it would be nice to be able to take a deep breath without coughing. It's the little things, you know?

I don't know... I've tried relaxation stuff before and I just can't seem to calm down. But I guess it's worth a shot. Just don't expect me to just sit there and breathe or anything like that. I need something a little more... tangible, I guess.

Yeah, okay... a walk might not be so bad. But what if the cravings are really strong? Like, when I'm at work and I'm stressed out... I don't know if I can just get up and walk around. My boss would think I'm crazy.
I guess that's doable. But what about when I get home? I smoke after dinner almost every day... I don't know how to break that habit.

I already brush my teeth, so that's not really an option. And chewing gum might help for a little while, but I know I'd just end up lighting a cigarette anyway. I need to find something else to do that's more meaningful, I guess.

My kid might actually be good for that. She's always checking in on me, making sure I'm doing okay. Yeah, that could work. She'd remind me to get back to my art and I'd be like, "Yeah, yeah, I'm working on it."

I don't know... maybe something small, like a nice dinner or a movie night with my family. Or, I don't know, a new set of paints or something. Something to make it feel like I've accomplished something.

motivation_reason: not wanting to change social dynamics

session_notes.note: I work at this dhaba for like 10 years now. And, um, I started smoking around 22 years old.
It's pretty chill. I take orders, cook food, and chat with customers. After the rush, when it's all quiet, we all sit around and smoke. It's just a thing we do.
Honestly, it makes me feel...left out, I guess. Like, I'm not one of the guys anymore if I don't smoke with them. It's hard to explain. I don't know if I want to change that.
I guess...I mean, it's not like I'm saying I want to quit forever right now, just trying something new. But, yeah, a phrase might help.
I don't know...They might think I'm being weak or something. But, I mean, I guess it's better than just quitting cold turkey and then being all distant.
I guess if I explained it like it's just me trying to be healthier, and it's not about us or our habits, they might understand. And if they're really my friends, they should support me, right?
My wife, maybe. She's always been pretty supportive, and she's been on me about quitting for years. I never thought I'd actually do it, so maybe she'll be happy if I finally try.
A journal might help me get everything straight in my head. It's hard to keep track of all the reasons I want to quit and all the reasons I don't right now.

motivation_reason: more than just having something to fidget with

session_notes.note: I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.

A lot of deadlines were due, and my team was under a lot of pressure. I had to work long hours and make some tough calls. It was stressful and I just felt overwhelmed, and somehow smoking just became a habit again.
I've seen those stress balls before, but I'm not sure it would work for me. I think it's about more than just having something to fidget with.

It's hard to put into words, but it's like smoking is a way for me to take a break, you know? It's a excuse to get out of the room or step away from my desk, even if it's just for a minute. And sometimes, when I'm really stressed, I just feel like I need a physical way to shake it off, I guess.

I guess so, yeah. Going for a walk is a good idea. I like that. But it's just that when I'm in the middle of a meeting or something, it's hard to excuse myself to go outside. And sometimes I feel like my coworkers would think it's weird if I started doing stretching exercises at my desk. I don't know, it just feels kinda awkward.
That might be doable. I can try that, I guess. But what if people think I'm not paying attention or that I'm being unprofessional? I don't know if I'm comfortable doing that in front of everyone.
Yeah, maybe that would help. It's not like I'm asking them to do anything, just that I need to take care of myself sometimes. But...I don't know, it still feels kinda personal, you know?

I've got a friend who's also trying to quit, but he's been struggling too. Maybe having both of us support each other could work. It would be nice to have someone to talk to who understands what I'm going through.
Yeah, I can do that. How about I try to make it to lunchtime without smoking? That's at least 5 hours.

motivation_reason: don't want to be that person all the time

session_notes.note: I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.
Regular, I don't know, just... regular cigarettes.
I guess that might be doable. But what about when I'm at my friends' places or my boyfriend's? They all smoke too.
They'd just think it's weird, or try to get me to join them. They wouldn't really understand. Plus, it's hard to say no to them. They smoke with me all the time.
My boyfriend's really understanding, he's always trying to help me. But even with him, it's hard to ask for help when I'm feeling overwhelmed. I feel like I'm already a burden to him with my anxiety.
That could work, I guess. But what if he thinks I'm just being anxious again and tries to talk me out of it? I don't want to be that person all the time.
I... get really uncomfortable. I start to feel like I'm going to freak out, like I'm going to embarrass myself in front of everyone. That's usually when I reach for a cigarette.

motivation_reason: stay active and feel good about myself
having something to look forward to

session_notes.note: I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.

Honestly, it was just a split second decision. I was in a social situation, and it felt like this one little thing, this one cigarette, was the easiest way to fit in and not rock the boat. I didn't really think about it, I just did it.

It's just... I feel like I've let myself down. I know I can quit, I've done it before, but it always slips away. And I'm tired of feeling like I'm not in control. Plus, my girlfriend's always on my case about it, and it's just getting old.

During the 2 years I quit, I found out I actually enjoyed hiking and biking. I started doing that a lot, and it really helped me stay active and feel good about myself. This time around, I was thinking maybe I could try to get back into that, and maybe even find some new activities that'll make quitting easier. But it's hard, you know, when you're used to smoking in social situations...

I guess that would be a good idea. But only if my girlfriend can be there too. She's always been supportive of me quitting, and it might be good for her to see me making an effort to try something new.

Yeah, that sounds like a good starting point. I mean, 15-20 minutes a day, that's doable. Plus, it's not just about the guitar, it's about having something to look forward to, you know? Something that's not smoking.

Rewarding myself sounds like a good way to stay motivated. Let me think for a second... if I can make it to a week without smoking, I'd like to treat myself to a new guitar pick. I've been eyeing this really cool one that's got a nice finish to it.

If I make it to a smoke-free week, I think I'd like to have a little celebration with my girlfriend. Maybe we could go out for dinner or see a movie, something low-key. And then, I could surprise her by taking her to this new hiking trail that just opened up near our house. It's something we've been meaning to check out, but haven't had a chance yet.

I think that's doable, but it's hard for me to put a time limit on it. Like, if I'm at a party and someone offers me a cigarette, I don't know if I can just say no right then and there. I think I need to find ways to make the delay feel more manageable, maybe by distracting myself with something else or talking to my girlfriend about how I'm feeling.

motivation_reason: want her to see me healthy and strong

session_notes.note: I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.

I don't know... it's just that my husband smokes too, and our friends all do, but when I'm around my daughter, I want to be a good role model. I want her to see me healthy and strong. Seeing me smoke all the time, it's just... it's not what I want her to see.

It's hard, yeah. When they're all smoking, it's just so normal for me to light up too. And they're all supportive, they say I'm doing great and they'll quit with me, but it doesn't feel like it. It feels like they're just being nice, you know?

I don't know... I've asked my husband to quit before and he's tried, but he gives up easily. I don't know if he'd be able to help me or make me feel worse. It's hard to ask someone for support when I know they're not really committed to quitting themselves.

Well, during my longest quit attempt, I actually found that using gum and going for short walks really helped me manage stress and cravings. It was just something to distract me from wanting to smoke. But I think what really fell apart was when I got into high-stress meetings at work. I just couldn't find a way to calm down and wait out the cravings.

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I could try keeping some gum in my bag or even a small stress ball or something. But what about when I'm driving? That's still a big trigger for me, and I don't think I can just pull over to take a walk in the middle of the road.

I've thought about listening to music or audiobooks while I drive, but I'm not sure if that would really help. I usually get a bunch of work calls or listen to the news, so I'm stuck with the stress and anxiety anyway. But maybe I could try to plan my route ahead of time, that way I could avoid areas with a lot of traffic and reduce my stress level before I even get in the car.

Actually, yeah... when I'm at home, I sometimes listen to calming music or nature sounds to help me wind down and relax. It does help take my mind off things, so maybe carrying an MP3 player or phone with some of that music in the car could work too.

I was thinking that during the 10 minutes, I could take some deep breaths, focus on my surroundings, and remind myself why I'm quitting. I could also try to think of other ways to cope with my stress instead of smoking. Like, if I'm in a meeting, I could take notes or ask questions to stay engaged. If I'm driving, I could take a different route or call a friend to talk.
I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.

Yeah, it's scary. A typical day for me is driving my auto-rickshaw. I usually chew gutkha during long shifts, when I'm stuck at red lights, or when I have some downtime between trips. It helps me stay awake and keeps my mouth busy, you know?

Taking water... I mean, I do drink water, but it's not like I have a specific habit of drinking at those moments. I'd try it, but what if I forget? I'm always on the move, it's hard to keep track. And gutkha is already there, it's just a pouch.

A label... I don't know, that might help. But what if it gets in the way or falls off? And what about when I have a lot of trips one after another? It's not always easy to drink water and then have to deal with the next passenger right away.

When it happens, I'll probably feel a bit annoyed, to be honest. It's gonna be like, jolted out of my usual state. But maybe I can use that feeling to my advantage. Instead of getting annoyed, I can just take a sip of water and think, "Okay, I'm doing this." Maybe that can help make it a habit.

I mean, I'll still have cravings for gutkha when the reminder goes off. But if I can associate drinking water with those moments, maybe it can become a substitute... not the same kind of fix, but at least something to do with my hands and my mouth. Plus, it's free and good for me, so...

I never thought of just sitting with the craving. It's like, I always try to get rid of it or distract myself from it. But if I stay with it... I don't know, maybe it'll start to feel less intense or maybe I'll realize it's not as important as I think it is.

I think that's a good question. I don't want to just be avoiding the craving, but actually understanding it. Maybe I can try to label it in my head - "I'm feeling restless and I want a fix" or something like that. And then I'll just sit with that feeling and see what's driving it. Is it boredom, or is it something else?

motivation_reason: improve my game for the team

session_notes.note: I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.

We usually hang out after college, have a few cigarettes, and catch up on our day. It's pretty normal for us. The team's performance has been declining lately, and our coach mentioned something about how I'm smoking too much. I don't know, maybe it's affecting me on the field or something.

Maybe if you told our coach about it and said it's part of our training or something, it wouldn't be so embarrassing. It's not like I'd want to be a different person, but if it helps the team, I'm willing to try.

If the coach says it's a good idea and it's for the team, I'd be more likely to give it a shot. He's always talking about how we need to work together and improve, so if he's behind it, I'd feel more confident. Plus, it wouldn't be just about me being weird; it'd be something we're all doing together.
It sounds kinda simple, but I don't know if I can just say no without feeling weird. What if they ask me why, and I don't have a good reason?

I can tell them it's because I want to improve my game for the team. That way, I'm not lying or anything, and it's related to why I want to quit in the first place. Maybe it'll even sound cool to them or something.

I'd say something like, "Nah, I'm trying to stay sharp for the team. I want to make sure I'm doing everything I can to help us win." It sounds pretty good in my head, but what if they don't believe me or think I'm being fake?

If you think the coach is behind me trying to quit, that would help a lot. If he's on my side, it'd show that it's not just me being weird, that it's something we're all working towards together. That would make me feel more confident, I think.

I'd say something like, "Coach, I know we've been struggling lately, and I want to be a part of the solution. I'm trying to quit smoking to improve my game, and I was wondering if you could help me with that. Your support would mean a lot to me, and I'd really appreciate it if you could be on my side."
It'd be a huge weight off my shoulders, I think. Knowing the coach has my back would give me so much more confidence to say no to cigarettes, and it would feel like I've got a lot less to lose.
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.
It's after I finish cooking, you know, when I have some free time, I usually reach for a bidi. And sometimes in the afternoons when I'm resting, I just get used to having one.
I do notice, it's like... my head feels a bit clearer, I think. But it's hard to remember that when I'm tired.
Okay... I'll try. But if it doesn't work, I don't know what else to do...
I think I'll keep it in my bag, so it's always with me... and maybe I can even write down some other things I'm grateful for, not just about the walks.
I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.
I guess so. It would be harder without them, but my wife really wants me to quit. What about my weekends when I drink? We can't just stop drinking overnight, can we?
Reducing the drinking might help, but it's hard to say. When I'm drinking, I just want a cigarette. It's like they go together for me.

Yeah, I think I can try that. It can't hurt to play some guitar again. But what about when I'm stressed at work? I usually smoke a few when I'm feeling overwhelmed. Do you have any ideas for dealing with work stress?
